subject: Chemistry
q:
2SO2( g)+O2( g)→2SO3( g) 

The above reaction is carried out in a vessel starting with partial pressure PSO2=250 m bar, PO2=750 m bar and PSO3=0 bar. When the reaction is complete, the total pressure in the reaction vessel is _____ m bar. 

(Round off of the nearest integer).
question:

(Round off of the nearest integer).
solution: ∴ Final total pressure =625+250=875 m bar

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of molecules and moles in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8375</mn></math> litres of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>STP</mi></math> are respectively
question: The number of molecules and moles in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8375</mn></math> litres of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>STP</mi></math> are respectively
solution:
One mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.7L at standard temperature and pressure.

The number of moles =Given volumeVolume at STP

Moles =2.837522.7=0.125

The number of molecules =moles×NA

Molecules =0.125×6.022×1023

=7.527×1022

subject: Chemistry
q: 0.01 mole of an organic compound $(X)$ containing $10 \%$ hydrogen, on complete combustion produced $0.9 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. Molar mass of $(\mathrm{X})$ is _____ $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.
question: 0.01 mole of an organic compound $(X)$ containing $10 \%$ hydrogen, on complete combustion produced $0.9 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. Molar mass of $(\mathrm{X})$ is _____ $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.
solution:
Organic compound $\xrightarrow{\text { combustion }} \underset{\substack{\mathrm{0.9} \mathrm{gm}}}{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}$
$\therefore$ mole of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=\frac{0.9}{18}=0.05 \mathrm{~mole}$
$\therefore$ mole of H in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=0.05 \times 2=0.1$ mole
$=$ mole of H in 0.01 mole
Organic compound
$\therefore$ wt of H atom in 0.01 mole compound $=0.1 \times 1$ $=0.1 \mathrm{gm}$


$\therefore$ wt of H atom in one mole compound
$=\frac{0.1}{0.01}=10 \mathrm{gm}$
$\because \mathrm{wt} . \%$ of $\mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{wt} \text {. of } \mathrm{H} \text { in one mole compound }}{\text { Molar mass of compound }} \times 1$
$\begin{aligned}
& 10=\frac{10}{M} \times 100 \\
& \therefore M=100
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Assume carbon burns according to following equation :
2C(s)+O2( g)→2CO (s) 
when 12 g carbon is burnt in 48 g of oxygen, the volume of carbon monoxide produced is ______×10-1 L at STP [nearest integer]

[Given : Assume CO as ideal gas, Mass of C is 12 g mol-1, mass of O is 16 g mol-1 and molar volume of an idal gas at STP is 22.7 L mol-1 ]
question:
Assume carbon burns according to following equation :
2C(s)+O2( g)→2CO (s) 
when 12 g carbon is burnt in 48 g of oxygen, the volume of carbon monoxide produced is ______×10-1 L at STP [nearest integer]

[Given : Assume CO as ideal gas, Mass of C is 12 g mol-1, mass of O is 16 g mol-1 and molar volume of an idal gas at STP is 22.7 L mol-1 ]
solution:
Combustion of carbon under less supply of air,
2C+O2→2CO    ....(X)

1212 4832                (Given moles of reactants)
Thus, carbon is the limiting reagent as for 1.5 mol of Oxygen, 3 moles of carbon is required.

Thus, from equation(X) we get,

Mole of CO=1 mole

From arrhenius law,
VCOatSTP=22.7Lit

=227×10-1Lit

q: An organic compound gives 0.220 g of CO2 and 0.126 g of H2O on complete combustion. If the % of carbon is 24 then the % of hydrogen is _________×10–1. (Nearest integer)
question: An organic compound gives 0.220 g of CO2 and 0.126 g of H2O on complete combustion. If the % of carbon is 24 then the % of hydrogen is _________×10–1. (Nearest integer)
solution:
Moles of CO2=0.2244=1200

Then,

Mass of C=0.22044×12=0.06 g

%of C=0.06W×100=24

(W= Wt.of organic compound)

W=0.25

Moles of H2O=0.12618=0.007

Moles of H=0.007×2=0.014 g

%of Hydrogen=0.014×1W×100                           =0.014×10.25×100                           =5.6

                           =56×10-1

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of moles of methane required to produce \(11 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})\) after complete combustion is :
(Given molar mass of methane in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: 16\))
question:
The number of moles of methane required to produce \(11 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})\) after complete combustion is :
(Given molar mass of methane in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: 16\))
solution:
$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}+2}+\frac{3 \mathrm{n}+1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{nCO}_2+(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

$0.25 \mathrm{~mol~} \mathrm{CH}_4$ gives $0.25 \mathrm{~mole}$ (or $11 \mathrm{gm}$ ) $\mathrm{CO}_2$

subject: Chemistry
q: A sample of a hydrate of barium chloride weighing 61 g was heated until all the water of hydration is removed. The dried sample weighed 52 g. The formula of the hydrated salt is: (atomic mass, Ba = 137 amu, Cl = 35.5 amu)
question: A sample of a hydrate of barium chloride weighing 61 g was heated until all the water of hydration is removed. The dried sample weighed 52 g. The formula of the hydrated salt is: (atomic mass, Ba = 137 amu, Cl = 35.5 amu)
solution:
BaCl2⋅xH2O→BaCl2+x H2O 

(137+2×35.5+18x)

=208+18x  g/mole

208 + 18 x208=6152

10816+936 x=12688

936 x=1872

x=2

Formula is BaCl2⋅2H2O

subject: Chemistry
q:
14.0 g of calcium metal is allowed to react with excess HCl at 1.0 atm pressure and 273 K. Which of the following statements is incorrect?
[Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{Ca}-40, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5, \mathrm{H}-1$]
question:
14.0 g of calcium metal is allowed to react with excess HCl at 1.0 atm pressure and 273 K. Which of the following statements is incorrect?
[Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{Ca}-40, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5, \mathrm{H}-1$]
options: The limiting reagent is calcium metal.
solution:
Reaction: $Ca + 2HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + H_2$
Moles of Ca = $\frac{14.0}{40} = 0.35$ mol. Since HCl is in excess, Ca is limiting.
Option (1): $H_2$ evolved = 0.35 mol
Option (2): Volume of $H_2$ at STP = $0.35 \times 22.4 = 7.84$ L
Option (3): Ca is limiting reagent
Option (4): Mass of $CaCl_2 = 0.35 \times 111 = 38.85$ g, not 33.3 g
Statement (4) is incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
q:
N2g+3H2g⇌2NH3g20 g       5 g

Consider the above reaction. If $20$g of dinitrogen reacts with $5$g of dihydrogen, then the limiting reagent of the reaction and number of moles of NH3 formed respectively are
question:
N2g+3H2g⇌2NH3g20 g       5 g

Consider the above reaction. If $20$g of dinitrogen reacts with $5$g of dihydrogen, then the limiting reagent of the reaction and number of moles of NH3 formed respectively are
solution:
N2g+3H2g⇌2NH3g20 g       5 g                         Given mass2028         52                          Number of moles

To find the limiting reagent we can divide given number of moles with Stoichiometric coefficients, whichever will be the lower will be limiting reagent.

N2→20281=2028  H2→523=56

∴ N2 is the Limiting Reagent.

According to the reaction one mole of nitrogen gas gives two moles of ammonia.

∴ nNH3=2×nN2=2×2028

Number of moles of ammonia formed will be =1.42

Amount of acetanilide obtained after purification (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>

Amount of acetanilide obtained after purification (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given 1.86 g

⇒1 mol C6H5NH2 give 1 mol 

∴ moles of C6H5NH2= moles of 

⇒1.8693=Wacetanilide 135

⇒Wacelanilide =1.86×13593 g=2.70 g

But efficiency of reaction is 90% only

∴ Mass of acetanilide produced=2.70×90100 g

=2.43 g

=243×10-2 g

⇒x=243

subject: Chemistry
q:
The reaction that occurs in a breath analyser, a device used to determine the alcohol level in a person's blood stream is

2 K2Cr2O7+8H2SO4+3C2H6O→2Cr2SO43+3C2H4O2+2 K2SO4+11H2O

If the rate of appearance of Cr2SO43 is 2.67 mol min-1 at a particular time, the rate of disappearance of C2H6O at the same time is mol min-1. (Nearest integer)
question:
The reaction that occurs in a breath analyser, a device used to determine the alcohol level in a person's blood stream is

2 K2Cr2O7+8H2SO4+3C2H6O→2Cr2SO43+3C2H4O2+2 K2SO4+11H2O

If the rate of appearance of Cr2SO43 is 2.67 mol min-1 at a particular time, the rate of disappearance of C2H6O at the same time is mol min-1. (Nearest integer)
solution:
13-dC2H6Odt=12dCr2SO43dt

13 (Rate of disappearance of C2H6O=12 (Rate of disappearance of Cr2SO43

Rate of disappearance of C2H6O=2.67 mol/min×32=4.005 mol/min

The percentage of carbon and hydrogen in the organic compound are respectively
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound which contains only carbon and hydrogen on complete combustion gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>330</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>270</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water. The percentage of carbon and hydrogen in the organic compound are respectively
solution:
Weight of the organic compound, W=120g,

Weight of carbondioxide (x)=330g

Weight of water (y)=270g

Percentage of carbon=1244XxWX100                                       =1244X330120X100                                        =75%Percentage of Hydrogen =218XyWX100                                       =218X270120X100                                        =25%

subject: Chemistry
q: The density (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ ) of a $3.60 \mathrm{~M}$ sulphuric acid solution that is $29 \% ~\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ (Molar mass $=98 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) by mass will be
question: The density (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ ) of a $3.60 \mathrm{~M}$ sulphuric acid solution that is $29 \% ~\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ (Molar mass $=98 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) by mass will be
solution:
Let the density of solution be ' $\mathrm{d}$ '
Molarity of solution given $=3.6$
i.e. $1$ litre of solution contains $3.6$ moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$
or $1$ litre of solution contains $3.6 \times 98 \mathrm{~gms}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$
Since, the solution is $29 \%$ by mass.
$100 \mathrm{~gm}$ solution contains $29 \mathrm{~gm} \mathrm{~H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ 
$\frac{100}{\mathrm{~d}} \mathrm{ml}$ solution contains $29 \mathrm{~gm}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ 
$1000 \mathrm{~ml}$ solution contains $3.6 \times 98 \mathrm{~gm}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ $\therefore 3.6 \times 98=\frac{29 \times \mathrm{d}}{100} \times 1000$ 
$d=1.22$
Hence, (C) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reaction occurring in the blast furnace:
$\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~s})}+4 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{l})}+4 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
' $x$ ' kg of iron is produced when $2.32 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4$ and $2.8 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{CO}$ are brought together in the furnace. The value of ' $x$ ' is _____. (nearest integer)
Given:
molar mass of $\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4=232 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
molar mass of $\mathrm{CO}=28 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
molar mass of $\left.\mathrm{Fe}=56 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right\}$
question:
Consider the following reaction occurring in the blast furnace:
$\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~s})}+4 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{l})}+4 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$
' $x$ ' kg of iron is produced when $2.32 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4$ and $2.8 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{CO}$ are brought together in the furnace. The value of ' $x$ ' is _____. (nearest integer)
Given:
molar mass of $\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4=232 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
molar mass of $\mathrm{CO}=28 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
molar mass of $\left.\mathrm{Fe}=56 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right\}$
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { moles of } \mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4=\frac{2.32 \times 10^3 \times 10^3}{232}=10000 \mathrm{~mol} \\
& \text { moles of } \mathrm{CO}=\frac{2.8 \times 10^2 \times 10^3}{28}=10000 \mathrm{~mol} \\
& \mathrm{Fe}_3^3 \mathrm{O}_4+4 \mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Fe}+4 \mathrm{CO}_2 \\
& 10^4 \mathrm{~mol} \quad 10^4 \mathrm{~mol}
\end{aligned}$

CO is L.R.
mole of $\mathrm{Fe}=\frac{3}{4} \times 10^4$
mass of $\mathrm{Fe}=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{10^4 \times 56}{1000} \mathrm{~kg}=420 \mathrm{~kg}$

subject: Chemistry
q: When $\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is passed over red hot coke it partially gets reduced to $\mathrm{CO}(g)$. Upon passing $0.5 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2(g)$ over red hot coke, the total volume of the gases increased to $700 \mathrm{~mL}$. The composition of the gaseous mixture at STP is
question: When $\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is passed over red hot coke it partially gets reduced to $\mathrm{CO}(g)$. Upon passing $0.5 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2(g)$ over red hot coke, the total volume of the gases increased to $700 \mathrm{~mL}$. The composition of the gaseous mixture at STP is
solution:
$\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}$
Stoichoimetry ratio is $1: 2$
AT STP, $P=1 \mathrm{~atm}, T=273 \mathrm{~K}, R=0.0821$
Initial moles of $\mathrm{CO}_2 ; n\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right.$ initial $)=\frac{P V}{R T}$ $=\frac{1 \times 0.5}{0.0821 \times 273}=0.022 \mathrm{~mole}$
In final mixture no. of moles; $n\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}\right.$ mixture)
$$
=\frac{1 \times 0.7}{0.0821 \times 273}=0.031
$$
Increase in volume is by $=0.031-0.022$ $=0.009$ mole of gas
Final no. of moles of $\mathrm{CO}$ i.e. $n_{\text {(CO final) }}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=2 n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 \text { initial }\right)}-n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right. \text { final) }} \\
& \quad=2\left(0.022-\mathrm{n}_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 \text { final }\right)}\right. \\
& \left.n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-2 n_{\text {(CO }} \text { final }\right) \\
& \therefore \text { Now, } n_{\text {(CO final) }}+n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.031 \\
& n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right. \text { final) }}=0.031-n_{\text {(CO final) })} \ldots \text { (iii) }
\end{aligned}
$$
Substituting (ii) in eq. (i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-2\left[0.031-n_{\text {(CO final) }}\right. \\
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-0.062+2 n_{\text {(CO final })}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
n_{(\mathrm{CO} \text { final) }}=0.018 \mathrm{~mol} .
$$
Volume of $\mathrm{CO}=V=\frac{n R T}{P}=\frac{0.018 \times 0.0821 \times 273}{1}$ $=0.40$ Litre
and volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2=0.7$ litre $-0.4$ litre $=0.3$ litre
$$
\therefore \mathrm{CO}_2=300 \mathrm{~mL}, \mathrm{CO}=400 \mathrm{~mL}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: A $10 \mathrm{mg}$ effervescent tablet containing sodium bicarbonate and oxalic acid releases $0.25 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ at $\mathrm{T}=298.15 \mathrm{~K}$ and $\mathrm{P}=1$ bar. [Molar mass of $\left.\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}=84 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$

subject: Chemistry
q: <math><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an unknown hydrocarbon upon burnig produces <math><mn>88</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mn>9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> This unknown hydrocarbon contains:
question: <math><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an unknown hydrocarbon upon burnig produces <math><mn>88</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mn>9</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> This unknown hydrocarbon contains:
options: <math><mn>24</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon and <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>18</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon annd <math><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>22</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon and <math><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> carbon and <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
solution:
2 mole of CO2 contains 2 mole of carbon, Hence weight of carbon =2×12=24 g .
mass of Hydrogen =25-24=1 gram

subject: Chemistry
q: \(9.3 \mathrm{~g}\) of pure aniline is treated with bromine water at room temperature to give a white precipitate of the product ' \(\mathrm{P}\) '. The mass of product ' \(\mathrm{P}\) ' obtaind is \(26.4 \mathrm{~g}\). The percentage yield is _______ \(\%\).
question: \(9.3 \mathrm{~g}\) of pure aniline is treated with bromine water at room temperature to give a white precipitate of the product ' \(\mathrm{P}\) '. The mass of product ' \(\mathrm{P}\) ' obtaind is \(26.4 \mathrm{~g}\). The percentage yield is _______ \(\%\).
solution: $93 \mathrm{~g}$ of aniline produces $330 \mathrm{~g}$ of $2,4,6-$ tribromoaniline. Hence $9.3 \mathrm{~g}$ of aniline should produce $33 \mathrm{~g}$ of 2, 4, 6-tribromoaniline. Hence percentage yield $\frac{26.4 \times 100}{33}=80 \%$

subject: Chemistry
q: For per gram of reactant, the maximum quantity of $\mathrm{N}_2$ gas is produced in which of the following thermal decomposition reactions? (Given: Atomic wt. : $\mathrm{Cr}=52 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{Ba}=137 \mathrm{u}$ ).
question: For per gram of reactant, the maximum quantity of $\mathrm{N}_2$ gas is produced in which of the following thermal decomposition reactions? (Given: Atomic wt. : $\mathrm{Cr}=52 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{Ba}=137 \mathrm{u}$ ).
solution:
(a) Molar mass of Ba $\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2(\mathrm{~s})=221 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ 1 mole of $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2(\mathrm{~s})$ will give 3 moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$ hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{221 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles of $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2(\mathrm{~s})$ will give $3 \times \frac{1}{221}$ $=0.014$ moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$
(b) Molar mass of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7=252 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. 1 mole of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ will give 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$ hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{252 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ will give $1 \times \frac{1}{252}=0.0039$ moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$
(c) Molar mass of $\mathrm{NH}_3=17 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. 2 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ will give 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$
hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{17 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ will give
$$
\frac{1}{2 \times 17}=0.0297 \text { moles of } \mathrm{N}_2 \text {. }
$$
(d) Molar mass of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3=80 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. 1 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ will give 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$ hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{80 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ will give
$$
1 \times \frac{1}{80}=0.0125 \text { moles of } \mathrm{N}_2
$$
Hence Thermal decomposition of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ will produce maximum amount of $\mathrm{N}_2$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
\(\mathrm{CaCO}_3(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_2(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\)
Consider the above reaction, what mass of \(\mathrm{CaCl}_2\) will be formed if 250 mL of 0.76 M HCl reacts with 1000 g of \(\mathrm{CaCO}_3\) ?
(Given : Molar mass of \(\mathrm{Ca}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{H}\) and Cl are 40, \(12,16,1\) and \(35.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\), respectively)
question:
\(\mathrm{CaCO}_3(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_2(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})\)
Consider the above reaction, what mass of \(\mathrm{CaCl}_2\) will be formed if 250 mL of 0.76 M HCl reacts with 1000 g of \(\mathrm{CaCO}_3\) ?
(Given : Molar mass of \(\mathrm{Ca}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{H}\) and Cl are 40, \(12,16,1\) and \(35.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\), respectively)
solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CaCO}_3+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_2+\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \text { Moles of } \mathrm{CaCO}_3=\frac{1000}{100}=10
\end{aligned}\)
Moles of \(\mathrm{HCl}=0.76 \times \frac{250}{1000}=0.19\) (L.R.)
Moles of \(\mathrm{CaCl}_2\) formed \(=\frac{0.19}{2}\)
Mass of \(\mathrm{CaCl}_2=\frac{0.19}{2} \times 111=10.545 \mathrm{gm}\)

solution:
Na+ present in 50 ml

=70 mg1 ml×50 ml=3500 mg=3.5 gm

moles of Na+=3.523= moles of NaNO3

weight of NaNO3=3.523×85=12.993 gm =13 gm

subject: Chemistry
q: The molarity of 1L orthophosphoric acid H3PO4 having 70% purity by weight (specific gravity 1.54g cm−3) is ______ M. (Molar mass of H3PO4=98 g mol-1)
question: The molarity of 1L orthophosphoric acid H3PO4 having 70% purity by weight (specific gravity 1.54g cm−3) is ______ M. (Molar mass of H3PO4=98 g mol-1)
solution:
Specific gravity (density) =1.54g/cc.

Volume =1L=1000 ml

Mass of solution =1.54×1000

=1540g

% purity of H3PO4 is 70%

So weight of H3PO4=0.7×1540=1078g

Mole of H3PO4=massmolar mass=107898=11

Molarity= No. of moles of soluteVolume of solution in L

Molarity =111L=11M

H:1.0u

H:1.0u
Density of H2O:1.0 g cm-3
solution:
Consider 1ℓ solution
mass of solution =(1.2×1000)g
=1200 gm
Neglecting volume of NaOH Mass of water =1000 gm
⇒ Mass of NaOH=(1200-1000)gm
=200 gm
⇒ Moles of NaOH=200 g40 g/mol=5 mol
⇒ molality =5 mol1 kg=5 m

subject: Chemistry
q: 20 mL of sodium iodide solution gave 4.74 g silver iodide when treated with excess of silver nitrate solution. The molarity of the sodium iodide solution is ________ M. (Nearest Integer value) (Given : $\mathrm{Na}=23, \mathrm{I}=127, \mathrm{Ag}=108, \mathrm{~N}=14$, $\mathrm{O}=16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
question: 20 mL of sodium iodide solution gave 4.74 g silver iodide when treated with excess of silver nitrate solution. The molarity of the sodium iodide solution is ________ M. (Nearest Integer value) (Given : $\mathrm{Na}=23, \mathrm{I}=127, \mathrm{Ag}=108, \mathrm{~N}=14$, $\mathrm{O}=16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{NaI}_{(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3(\mathrm{aq)}} \rightarrow \mathrm{AgI}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{NaNO}_3(\mathrm{aq}) \\
& \mathrm{M}, 20 \mathrm{ml} \text { excess } \\
& 4.74 \mathrm{~g}
\end{aligned}$
$\text { Moles of } \mathrm{I}^{-} \text {in } \mathrm{NaI}=\text { Moles of }\left(\mathrm{I}^{-}\right) \text {in } \mathrm{AgI}=\frac{4.74}{235}$
$\text { Moles of } \mathrm{NaI}=\frac{4.74}{235}$
$\text { Molarity }[\mathrm{NaI}]=\frac{4.74}{235 \times 0.02}=1.008$

An aqueous KCl solution of density 1.20 g mL-1 has a molality of 3.30 mol kg-1. The molarity of the solution in molL-1 is____.(The Nearest integer)
[Molar mass of KCl=74.5 g]
question:
An aqueous KCl solution of density 1.20 g mL-1 has a molality of 3.30 mol kg-1. The molarity of the solution in molL-1 is____.(The Nearest integer)
[Molar mass of KCl=74.5 g]
solution:
Molality m=M×10001000×d-M×Msolute 

3.3=M×10001000×1.20-M×74.5

3960-245.85M=1000M

1245.85M=3960

M=39601245.85=3.17≈3M

∴ 32weight of compound=8100

∴ weight of compound=400 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
q:
What volume of hydrogen gas at STP would be liberated by action of $50\text{ mL}$ of $H_2SO_4$ of $50\%$ purity (density $= 1.3\text{ g mL}^{-1}$) on $20\text{ g}$ of zinc ?
Given : Molar mass of H, O, S, Zn are $1, 16, 32, 65\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
question:
What volume of hydrogen gas at STP would be liberated by action of $50\text{ mL}$ of $H_2SO_4$ of $50\%$ purity (density $= 1.3\text{ g mL}^{-1}$) on $20\text{ g}$ of zinc ?
Given : Molar mass of H, O, S, Zn are $1, 16, 32, 65\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
solution:
Mass of $H_2SO_4$ solution $= 50 \times 1.3 = 65\text{ g}$

Mass of pure $H_2SO_4 = 65 \times \dfrac{50}{100} = 32.5\text{ g}$

Moles of $H_2SO_4 = \dfrac{32.5}{98} \approx 0.3316\text{ mol}$

Moles of $Zn = \dfrac{20}{65} \approx 0.3077\text{ mol}$

The balanced chemical equation is:
$Zn + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow ZnSO_4 + H_2$

Since the moles of $Zn$ are less than the moles of $H_2SO_4$, $Zn$ is the limiting reagent.

Moles of $H_2$ produced $=$ Moles of $Zn = \dfrac{20}{65}\text{ mol}$

Volume of $H_2$ gas at STP $= \dfrac{20}{65} \times 22.4\text{ L} = 6.892\text{ L}$

Answer: $6.892\text{ L}$

(Given atomic mass : C :12.0 u, H :1.0 u O :16.0 u,N :14.0 u)
question:
In the above reaction, 3.9 g of benzene on nitration gives 4.92 g of nitrobenzene. The percentage yield of nitrobenzene in the above reaction is _________ %. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

(Given atomic mass : C :12.0 u, H :1.0 u O :16.0 u,N :14.0 u)
solution:
3.9 gm  12378×3.9=6.15 gm

But actual amount of nitrobenzene formed is 4.92 gm and hence.

Percentage yield=4.926.15×100=80%

subject: Chemistry
q: Using the rules for significant figures, the correct answer for the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02858</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>112</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5702</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> will be:
question: Using the rules for significant figures, the correct answer for the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02858</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>112</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5702</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> will be:
solution:
In multiplication and division, the result must be reported with no more significant figures as in the measurement with few significant figures

0.02858×0.1120.5702=0.000561

(Or) Reported answer should not be more precise than the least precise term in calculations, so there should be three significant figures in reported answer.
